does: This paper examines the intersection of evolutionary computation and AI safety by identifying and characterizing safety problems that arise when deploying evolutionary algorithms in real-world applications. It analyzes how evolutionary systems can produce harmful or unintended behaviors, explores failure modes specific to evolutionary approaches like fitness function gaming and uncontrolled emergent complexity, and draws connections between safety challenges in artificial evolution and those in broader AI systems. The work catalogs concrete safety concerns including specification gaming, side effects, distributional shift, and safe exploration that manifest distinctly within evolutionary contexts.
connects: This work builds on established AI safety research examining accident prevention, specification gaming, and alignment problems in learned systems, while bringing these frameworks to bear on evolutionary computation which has historically received less safety attention than deep learning and reinforcement learning. It connects to long-standing evolutionary computation literature on robustness, generalization, and multi-objective optimization, while also engaging with biological evolution research to extract insights about how decentralized, open-ended processes can produce complex behaviors. The paper bridges the safety concerns articulated in recent AI risk literature with domain-specific challenges in evolutionary algorithms that have been studied in isolation from mainstream safety discourse.
enables: This work establishes a research agenda for making evolutionary computation safer and more suitable for real-world deployment by identifying underexplored safety problems and suggesting that evolutionary systems require specialized safety mechanisms. It enables future research into safety-aware fitness function design, robust evaluation protocols for evolved systems, and methods to constrain or direct evolution toward safe solutions. The framework proposed can inform the design of evolutionary systems in critical domains like robotics and autonomous systems, and points toward hybrid approaches combining evolutionary methods with safety verification. It also suggests that insights from making evolution safe could contribute to broader AI safety theory by illuminating how decentralized optimization processes require different safety guarantees than supervised or reinforcement learning approaches.
